critic_raw:
GAN2Shape's core methodology relies on a critical assumption: that the latent space of a pre-trained StyleGAN2 encodes sufficient 3D structural information to enable recovery of 3D shape from 2D image generators. This assumption is plausible but not formally validated — there is no guarantee that variations in the latent vector w faithfully sample all viewpoints and lighting conditions needed to triangulate geometry, rather than confounding 3D structure with learned texture or lighting shortcuts within the GAN's training distribution.

The method is validated on a narrow set of categories (human faces, cats, cars, buildings) that are all well-represented in StyleGAN2's training data and are highly structured object classes. Generalization claims to 'various datasets' are therefore only demonstrated within the specific domain biases of StyleGAN2's training set. There is no evaluation on categories where StyleGAN2 has weaker prior geometry knowledge, which would be necessary to support broader generalization claims.

A fundamental confound arises from using the same StyleGAN2 model for both the image generation process (multi-view synthesis by varying w) and the 3D shape recovery. The recovered 3D shapes may reflect the geometry biases baked into StyleGAN2's training rather than any independent geometric inference. Without a cross-model validation (recovering shapes with one GAN family and validating against another), it is impossible to disentangle recovered 'shape' from StyleGAN2's implicit shape priors.

The evaluation of 3D shape quality depends on the quality of viewpoint and lighting samples generated by the GAN, but the adequacy of this sampling is not characterized. If the GAN does not produce reliable novel viewpoints (e.g., extreme angles, unusual lighting), the recovered geometry will be systematically biased toward the camera angles prevalent in training. Quantitative 3D shape evaluation metrics and comparison to supervised or ground-truth 3D baselines are needed to validate the actual geometric accuracy of the recovered shapes.
all_issues_raised: Core assumption that StyleGAN2 latent space encodes sufficient 3D structure is unvalidated and potentially conflated with texture/lighting shortcuts
Evaluation limited to highly structured, GAN-training-dominant categories (faces, cats, cars, buildings); no generalization to underrepresented categories
Circular confound: same StyleGAN2 used for multi-view synthesis and shape recovery, making it impossible to separate recovered geometry from StyleGAN2's implicit 3D priors
Adequacy of viewpoint and lighting sampling from the GAN is uncharacterized, leading to potential systematic geometric bias toward training-prevalent angles
No quantitative 3D shape evaluation against ground-truth geometry or supervised 3D baselines to validate actual geometric accuracy
Conflation of latent space correlations with true 3D geometric understanding — latent traversal may capture 2D rendering artifacts rather than genuine viewpoint changes
Selection bias in evaluation datasets: restricted to categories (faces, cars, cats, buildings) where StyleGAN2 has highly structured representations, limiting generalizability claims
Errors and biases from the pretrained StyleGAN2 generator propagate into 3D reconstructions without decomposition, making it impossible to isolate the algorithm's contribution
Absence of quantitative 3D ground truth evaluation; validation relies on visual inspection and proxy metrics rather than multi-view geometry benchmarks
Insufficient treatment of lighting/viewpoint disentanglement — these factors are co-entangled in natural images and the paper does not rigorously verify that traversal paths isolate them
Core assumption that StyleGAN2 latent space encodes disentangled 3D variation is treated as given rather than empirically verified per model/dataset
Evaluation restricted to categories (faces, cats, cars, buildings) where StyleGAN2 training data is most abundant and structured, overstating generalizability
Absence of quantitative 3D evaluation against ground-truth geometry; results rely primarily on visual inspection of recovered shapes
Circularity risk: latent w-traversal may not faithfully generate perspective-accurate multi-view images, yet these are used as pseudo-ground-truth for 3D recovery
No diagnostic criteria or metrics for detecting failure cases where the method's 3D assumptions break down
